title: “I See What You Did There”: Can Large Vision-Language Models Understand Multimodal Puns?
venue: Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers), pages 9786–9805
research_questions: RQ1 – How effectively can VLMs recognize multimodal puns against non-puns?
RQ2 – To what extent can VLMs explain puns?
RQ3 – How can we enhance VLMs’ understanding of puns?

datasets_used.name: MultiPun
datasets_used.size: 445 positive pun instances and 890 negative samples

datasets_used.name: MultiPun
datasets_used.size: 666 training samples for Pun-Tuning; 669 held-out test samples

methods_proposed.name: MULTIPUN construction pipeline
methods_proposed.summary: A pipeline that generates homophonic and homographic multimodal pun tuples from CMU Pronouncing Dictionary and WordNet, uses GPT-4o to generate captions, image descriptions, and interpretations, and uses GPT-image-1 to create images. It adds adversarial non-pun distractors through Explicative Substitution and Random Substitution, followed by diversity filtering and human verification.

methods_proposed.name: Biased-prompt evaluation suite
methods_proposed.summary: A three-task evaluation suite for detection, localization, and explanation that asks each question in both biased-to-pun and biased-to-non-pun forms. The design is intended to separate actual pun understanding from affirmative or prompt-induced bias.

methods_proposed.name: Pun-CoT
methods_proposed.summary: A prompt-level method that enforces three-stage verification: visual grounding, lexical anchoring, and cross-modal verification. It is designed to prevent pun keyword hallucination, phonetic hallucination, semantic hallucination, and visual object hallucination.

methods_proposed.name: Pun-Tuning
methods_proposed.summary: A model-level fine-tuning approach using MULTIPUN positive and negative samples, high-quality responses, and both biased-to-pun and biased-to-non-pun prompts. It targets over-interpretation, imprecise explanations, and prompt sensitivity.

humor_theories.detail: The paper operationalizes a linguistic taxonomy of puns into homophonic puns and homographic puns, with a multimodal pun tuple P=<w_p,w_a,S_p,S_a> representing the caption word, alternative word, literal visual sense, and figurative/contextual sense.

evaluation_methods.detail: Pun recognition is evaluated with TPR, TNR, F1-score, changes in TPR/TNR under prompt bias shifts, and Cohen’s Kappa.
Pun component verification uses mention ratio for ground-truth pun word w_p and alternative word w_a in localization and explanation outputs.
An advanced LLM judge performs pairwise comparisons between VLM-generated explanations and ground-truth explanations, labeling each as Win, Tie, or Loss.

Three graduate-student annotators verify generated positive and negative samples for image quality, visual-textual coherence, ambiguity presence, and naturalness; samples are retained if at least 2 of 3 annotators accept them.

human_samples.population: graduate students from the authors’ institution with prior experience in NLP research; aged 20-28; two male and one female doctoral students in computer science
human_samples.recruitment: Recruited from the authors’ institution; compensated at $25 USD/hour for approximately 8 hours per participant; provided informed consent
human_samples.observations: All generated samples, positive and negative, were independently evaluated; samples were retained if at least 2 out of 3 annotators agreed on acceptance; Fleiss’ Kappa was 0.78
human_samples.detail: Annotators checked image quality, visual-textual coherence, ambiguity presence, and naturalness. Annotations were anonymized and used only for academic research.

judges.detail: Three graduate-student annotators verified dataset samples and determined acceptance by at least 2-of-3 agreement.
The authors manually verified image-description alignment and refined prompts to regenerate images when mismatches occurred.

judges.name: TPR, TNR, F1, ΔTPR, ΔTNR, Cohen’s Kappa, mention ratio
judges.detail: Automatic metrics determine the main pun recognition and component verification results.

judges.name: advanced LLM judge
judges.detail: The paper does not name the judge model; it compares model-generated explanations with MULTIPUN ground-truth explanations and outputs Win/Tie/Loss labels.

generation_selection.detail: GPT-4o generates caption, image description, and interpretation for each tuple, and GPT-image-1 creates the visual scene. The paper reports manual verification and prompt refinement/regeneration for mismatches, but not the number of candidates or samples per tuple.
Explicative Substitution and Random Substitution negatives are generated with prompt templates and then human-verified; the paper does not report candidate counts or sampling parameters.

generation_selection.scope: VLM outputs for detection, localization, and explanation
generation_selection.n: All experiments are run three times, and the reported results are averages.
generation_selection.detail: The repeated runs are averaged; no best-of-N generation or selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-5.1
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source VLM accessed via official API.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a closed-source VLM; GPT-4o is also used in positive sample generation, but the generation version is not separately specified.

model_provenance.name: Gemini-3-Pro
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source VLM accessed via official API.

model_provenance.name: Claude Sonnet 4.5
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source VLM accessed via official API.

model_provenance.name: Llama-4-Scout-17B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-13B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source checkpoint hosted on a vLLM server.

model_provenance.name: GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking
model_provenance.detail: The model is evaluated and described as an open-source reasoning-based VLM, but the checkpoint is not listed in Table 8.

model_provenance.name: GPT-image-1
model_provenance.detail: Used to generate visual scenes from image descriptions for MULTIPUN.

model_provenance.name: text-embedding-3-large
model_provenance.detail: Used for embedding-based diversity filtering of generated samples.

model_provenance.name: advanced LLM judge
model_provenance.detail: Used for pairwise explanation comparison, but the paper does not name the model or version.

inference_budgets.scope: Main VLM evaluation across detection, localization, and explanation
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: two prompt variants per task (biased-to-pun and biased-to-non-pun); all experiments are run three times
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Open-source models are evaluated on two NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs; closed-source models are accessed via official APIs.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports repeated runs and hardware/access mode but not temperature, top-p, max tokens, cost, or exact inference dates.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: GPT-4o is prompted to generate three distinct components for each tuple; the image description is subsequently fed into GPT-image-1
inference_budgets.detail: The paper reports manual prompt refinement and image regeneration for mismatches but not the number of regeneration attempts or sampling settings.
An advanced LLM judge compares generated explanations to ground-truth explanations; the exact judge model, prompt budget, and call counts are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Training uses both biased-to-pun and biased-to-non-pun prompt variants; evaluation is performed on the held-out test set (669 samples).
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: batch size 4 per A100 GPU, learning rate 2e-5, AdamW optimizer, linear warmup (100 steps) followed by cosine decay, weight decay 0.01, gradient clipping (max norm 1.0), FP16 mixed precision for 3 epochs
inference_budgets.detail: These are fine-tuning hyperparameters rather than inference sampling parameters; inference sampling settings remain unreported.

human_baselines.description: The paper uses human verification for dataset quality but does not report human performance as a baseline on the detection, localization, or explanation tasks.
human_baselines.detail: Ground-truth explanations are compared against model explanations, but no human-generated explanation baseline under matched conditions is reported.

mitigations: MULTIPUN is newly constructed in the paper rather than a legacy public benchmark.
The Pun-Tuning experiments use a split that the paper says ensures no test samples leak into training.
contamination_risk.basis: The benchmark is newly introduced, which lowers legacy benchmark contamination risk. However, GPT-4o is used to generate MULTIPUN content and is also evaluated, no decontamination test is reported for proprietary models, and inference dates are not provided.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Claims about pairwise explanation quality, such as ground-truth explanations generally outperforming VLM-generated explanations and Pun-Tuning increasing win rates, depend on an unnamed advanced LLM judge.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: No human validation of the LLM judge’s Win/Tie/Loss decisions is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The main recognition and F1 claims are computed with automatic metrics against dataset labels, but explanation-quality comparisons use an LLM judge.

reporting_gaps: The advanced LLM judge used for pairwise explanation comparison is not named and has no reported version or snapshot.
No inference dates are reported for API-based models.
Sampling parameters such as temperature, top-p, max tokens, and decoding settings are not reported for evaluated VLMs, dataset generation, or the LLM judge.
The checkpoint/configuration for GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking is not listed in the open-source model configuration table.
The paper states that fine-tuning experiments are limited to three open-source models, but Table 4 reports Pun-Tuning results for four models including Llama-4 Scout-17B.
Exact candidate counts and regeneration counts for dataset generation are not reported.
The pairwise comparison figures are discussed qualitatively, but exact Win/Tie/Loss values are not tabulated in the provided text.
Blindness of human verification annotations is not reported.

headline_results.finding: MULTIPUN contains 445 positive pun instances and 890 adversarial negative samples, for 1,335 total samples.
headline_results.value: 445 positive pun instances; 890 negative samples; 1,335 total samples

headline_results.finding: GPT-5.1 achieves the highest reported explanation-task F1 among the evaluated models, with 0.804 on homophonic puns and 0.757 on homographic puns.
Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct identifies almost every homophonic detection input as a pun, producing near-perfect TPR but extremely low TNR.
LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-13B shows extreme prompt-induced bias in homophonic detection when the prompt shifts from biased-to-pun to biased-to-non-pun.
Pun-CoT improves Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking on homophonic explanation by raising F1 and non-pun rejection.
Pun-Tuning substantially improves LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-13B explanation-task F1 on both pun types.

key_findings: Most evaluated VLMs achieve high TPR but low TNR, indicating that they often misclassify adversarial non-puns as puns.
Open-source models are more sensitive to prompt wording, while closed-source models such as GPT-4o and Gemini-3-Pro show more consistent behavior across biased prompt variants.
Requiring explanations improves rejection of non-puns but can reduce TPR because models sometimes discard valid puns when they fail to justify the mechanism.
Models usually identify the caption pun word w_p, but retrieving the alternative word w_a is the bottleneck for homophonic puns.
Reasoning-based variants have mixed effects: they help larger models in some settings but can worsen hallucination in smaller models.
Both Pun-CoT and Pun-Tuning improve F1 and non-pun rejection, and the paper reports an average F1-score improvement of 16.5%.
limitations: The benchmark focuses exclusively on English puns.
The evaluation covers 11 representative VLMs, but newer models may behave differently.
The paper states that fine-tuning experiments are limited to three open-source models due to computational constraints.
The adversarial negative strategies may not cover all possible failure modes.
Pairwise explanation-quality conclusions rely on an unnamed advanced LLM judge without reported human validation.
The dataset is synthetic and generated partly with GPT-4o, which is also one of the evaluated models.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that MULTIPUN data is generated using publicly available text-to-image models and LLMs under their intended purposes and licenses, contains no personally identifiable information or real individuals, and acknowledges dual-use risks in which improved linguistic manipulation could be used for social engineering or propaganda.

artifacts.other: A footnote states that the code is available, but the URL is not present in the provided transcription.
